This creates the foundation for 2x–5x growth, and only at that point does AI actually make sense.

column9:
Hi NAME,
Lately I’ve been noticing a clear split among founders when it comes to AI.

Some are using AI "playing not to lose", speeding up emails, summarizing docs, adding small efficiencies to the same operating model.
It feels productive, but nothing fundamentally changes.

Others are using AI offensively.
They’re stepping back and asking:
“If AI is now part of how work gets done, what parts of this business should be rebuilt entirely?”

The difference between these two approaches isn’t obvious on the P&L yet.
But operationally, it’s already there: in decision speed, ownership, and how much the business still depends on the founder.

This is the space I work in.
If this way of thinking resonates, happy to compare notes.

Regards,
Den

column3:
Hi NAME 👋 great to be connected

One pattern I see again and again with tech founders:

A business doesn’t stop growing because of the market.
It stops when the operating system can’t keep up with decisions.

At that point, the founder becomes the bottleneck.
Not because of control,
but because the system can’t keep up.

Tools without operating logic only accelerate chaos.
Process without the right systems hits a ceiling.
Scale only happens when both are synchronized.

Does this resonate?

That’s the work I do as a Fractional COO.
I help founders rebuild the business operating system.
So execution catches up with decisions.
And growth becomes repeatable again.

Open to a 15-minute call to quickly see
where your business if stuck right now
or where momentum is being lost?

Hi NAME,
I’m currently working as a Fractional COO with tech/AI companies where growth is stuck and the founder has become the main operational bottleneck.

What I do is simple but hard: I step in and remove the founder from daily fire-fighting by building structure, metrics, and a clear decision system — so the company can finally scale without burning down the team.

Your profile/company caught my attention.
Would a direct 15-minute conversation make sense?

column5:
Hi NAME 👋,
one last quick note — then I’ll step back.

When I come in as a COO, founders usually start earning more—not because we “motivate the team,” but because the company finally gets an operating system instead of chaos.

That means:
➖ sales stop depending on the founder
➖ marketing spend becomes efficient and transparent
➖ hiring mistakes disappear and the talent bar rises
➖ delivery stabilizes and margins improve
➖ teams execute without escalation
➖ decisions happen faster with less noise
➖ weak processes get replaced by scalable ones
➖ and the company starts running on structure, not heroics

This is the work behind Digital Gateway OS™ — the operating system I’m building for tech companies that are scaling faster than their internal structure.

If this resonates even slightly, happy to talk.
If not, no problem — I’ll step back.

Hi Name 
Great to be connected 🤝

I thought you might find it useful to see what I specialize in. Recently, we built a system for a CMO using 7 AI agents that now handle about 90% of daily marketing tasks:

➖ Content Strategist Agent - plans campaigns and messaging.
➖ Copywriting Agent - writes posts, emails, and ad copy in brand tone.
➖ Design Agent - creates visuals and layouts.
➖ Analytics Agent - tracks performance and insights.
➖ Community Agent - engages with comments and DMs.
➖ SEO Agent - optimizes content and keywords automatically.
➖ AdOps Agent - manages ads and budget allocations.

Now the CMO simply manages the agents, instead of hiring 3–7 people for each role.

The next step for us is to expand the same approach into support, sales, and HR, and soon, even C-level agents that help with strategy and PMF decisions.

Would you like to see how this works in practice?
